bins.name: Flexible load service and interconnection
bins.desc: Antora advocates for regulatory frameworks that recognize and enable flexible, curtailable loads to interconnect without capacity matching requirements. Current models treating flexible loads like inflexible ones block deployment and reduce system benefits. PALS demonstrates a viable model, and all RTOs should offer comparable permanent non-firm service.
quotes: load flexibility can critically help to address the challenge of large load growth by increasing transmission and generation capacity utilization, putting downward pressure on rates, and by allowing the grid to accommodate new loads while infrastructure to serve them is being built

existing service models sharply constrain the commercial viability of flexible load development, and in particular development of highly flexible loads like TES, by treating them the same as inflexible loads

Antora urges the Commission, in crafting its remedy in this proceeding, to go beyond these recent reforms to require all transmission providers to: (1) offer permanent non-firm transmission service; (2) at rates reflecting actual use that is consistent with cost causation principles; (3) with curtailment requirements for non-firm load integrated into the interconnection study process and resource adequacy requirements

Highly flexible large loads face substantial interconnection barriers where, as in SPP, the RTO requires a one-to-one matching of non-coincident peak demand with new Designated Network Resources (DNRs). Highly flexible, curtailable large loads can avoid peaks and are inherently price sensitive, meaning they only operate when there is abundant low-cost energy generation available. An artificial DNR requirement is prohibitively expensive, unnecessary given the real-world operation of curtailable large loads like TES, and acts as a roadblock to new beneficial flexible loads
low load-factor, price-sensitive, highly flexible loads have no current mechanism for gaining transmission access without Designated Network Resources that will not be used or needed

MISO allows LSEs to serve new off-peak load without a 1:1 DNR match, and removes Load Modifying Resources from capacity calculations due to interruptibility. This more flexible approach is one reason Antora's first large scale commercial project, which we announced is in commissioning this week, is in MISO, and not SPP

Price Adaptive Load Service (PALS). SPP staff are currently developing the PALS proposal and plan to bring it to the stakeholder process for approvals later this year. PALS would create a long-term, non-firm transmission service enabling flexible, price-sensitive loads to interconnect without matching capacity

Full Curtailability: PALS loads are always dispatchable to zero. They are curtailed during reliability events, emergency conditions, and meaningful system peaks. SPP or the Transmission Owner can shut down PALS load immediately

Offer a permanent, long-term, non-firm transmission service for fully-curtailable, price-responsive loads in addition to temporary or conditional service for loads wishing to move to firm. Recognizing that some customers such as Antora can limit withdrawals whether or not co-located with generation, this service should be available to any customer capable of meeting the necessary withdrawal limitations, whether or not co-located
This opportunity is being hamstrung by frameworks that assume all loads operate at full capacity around the clock

PALS offers a sound model—protecting reliability through full curtailability, limiting price impacts through a bid cap, avoiding resource adequacy complications by design, and charging based on actual use
every jurisdictional RTO and transmission owner should be required to offer comparable, permanent, cost-causation-based non-firm service for flexible loads

bins.name: Cost-causation rate design for flexible loads
bins.desc: Antora argues that transmission rates for flexible loads must reflect actual usage via cost-causation principles rather than peak-based or capacity-matching models. Annual reservation charges in CHILLS and DNR requirements violate cost-causation, imposing unjust and unreasonable costs. PALS' hourly MWh rates align costs with actual load patterns and reduce existing customer charges.
quotes: Antora urges the Commission, in crafting its remedy in this proceeding, to go beyond these recent reforms to require all transmission providers to: (1) offer permanent non-firm transmission service; (2) at rates reflecting actual use that is consistent with cost causation principles; (3) with curtailment requirements for non-firm load integrated into the interconnection study process and resource adequacy requirements
Antora estimates 1 GW of flexible load projects could generate $65.4 million in additional transmission revenues, reducing existing customer NITS fees by 1-12% depending on the zone in SPP

Requiring such loads to procure generation capacity as though they consume around the clock yields unjust and unreasonable costs, contradicts cost-causation principles, and unduly discriminates against such loads by treating differently-situated customers the same

CHILLS requires annual reservations. This means that an Antora thermal storage site would have to pay for transmission at the peak and off peak non-firm point to point rates (which can be very expensive) despite the fact that it would only use power ~1/3 of the time, and only during hours when LMPs are at their lowest

This approach is unjust and unreasonable, inconsistent with the Commission's cost causation principle, and unduly discriminatory for treating differently situated loads similarly—as well as prohibitively expensive for highly price sensitive industrial loads like thermal storage

Non-Firm Transmission Charges: Per-MWh billing at hourly non-firm rates (Zonal Annual Transmission Revenue Requirement/Transmission Zone Peak Load/8760) reflect the true cost causation profile of curtailable loads; directly supporting NITS costs reduction for existing ratepayers
Set transmission charges based on cost-causation principles reflecting actual use that is consistent with how RTOs already bill other firm customers

PALS offers a sound model—protecting reliability through full curtailability, limiting price impacts through a bid cap, avoiding resource adequacy complications by design, and charging based on actual use
every jurisdictional RTO and transmission owner should be required to offer comparable, permanent, cost-causation-based non-firm service for flexible loads

bins.name: Interconnection and system planning improvements
bins.desc: Antora urges that interconnection studies and system planning integrate the curtailable, non-firm nature of flexible loads so assumptions reflect actual operating profiles. Planning should also account for full benefits flexible loads provide, including avoided transmission upgrades, rather than treating them as standard demand-side resources.
quotes: Antora urges the Commission, in crafting its remedy in this proceeding, to go beyond these recent reforms to require all transmission providers to: (1) offer permanent non-firm transmission service; (2) at rates reflecting actual use that is consistent with cost causation principles; (3) with curtailment requirements for non-firm load integrated into the interconnection study process and resource adequacy requirements
Integrate the non-firm, curtailable nature of flexible loads into the interconnection study process so assumptions reflect actual operating profiles
Study the full range of benefits that efficiently-sited highly flexible loads can provide to the system, including by avoiding the need for new transmission upgrades

bins.name: Southwest Power Pool experience and reforms
bins.desc: Antora credits SPP for pioneering efforts to accommodate flexible loads through CHILLS and emerging PALS, but finds CHILLS insufficient due to temporary structure and annual reservation rates. SPP's DNR requirement has driven deployment to MISO. PALS holds significant promise and should be completed promptly and serve as a model for national requirements.
quotes: Antora has identified a 6+ GW opportunity in SPP and 10+ GW nationally of immediately cost effective, viable, and highly-flexible thermal load opportunities based on current wholesale energy prices, which can be sited at existing facilities in biofuels, chemicals, food processing, and agriculture
Antora estimates 1 GW of flexible load projects could generate $65.4 million in additional transmission revenues, reducing existing customer NITS fees by 1-12% depending on the zone in SPP

Highly flexible large loads face substantial interconnection barriers where, as in SPP, the RTO requires a one-to-one matching of non-coincident peak demand with new Designated Network Resources (DNRs). Highly flexible, curtailable large loads can avoid peaks and are inherently price sensitive, meaning they only operate when there is abundant low-cost energy generation available. An artificial DNR requirement is prohibitively expensive, unnecessary given the real-world operation of curtailable large loads like TES, and acts as a roadblock to new beneficial flexible loads

MISO allows LSEs to serve new off-peak load without a 1:1 DNR match, and removes Load Modifying Resources from capacity calculations due to interruptibility. This more flexible approach is one reason Antora's first large scale commercial project, which we announced is in commissioning this week, is in MISO, and not SPP
CHILLS is conditional and temporary, only available for a seven-year term, after which the customer must transition to firm service

CHILLS requires annual reservations. This means that an Antora thermal storage site would have to pay for transmission at the peak and off peak non-firm point to point rates (which can be very expensive) despite the fact that it would only use power ~1/3 of the time, and only during hours when LMPs are at their lowest

Price Adaptive Load Service (PALS). SPP staff are currently developing the PALS proposal and plan to bring it to the stakeholder process for approvals later this year. PALS would create a long-term, non-firm transmission service enabling flexible, price-sensitive loads to interconnect without matching capacity

Non-Firm Transmission Charges: Per-MWh billing at hourly non-firm rates (Zonal Annual Transmission Revenue Requirement/Transmission Zone Peak Load/8760) reflect the true cost causation profile of curtailable loads; directly supporting NITS costs reduction for existing ratepayers

bins.name: MISO flexible load accommodations
bins.desc: MISO allows new off-peak loads without 1:1 DNR matching and removes interruptible Load Modifying Resources from capacity calculations. This flexible approach has attracted Antora's first commercial TES project to commissioning in MISO rather than SPP.
quotes: MISO allows LSEs to serve new off-peak load without a 1:1 DNR match, and removes Load Modifying Resources from capacity calculations due to interruptibility. This more flexible approach is one reason Antora's first large scale commercial project, which we announced is in commissioning this week, is in MISO, and not SPP

bins.name: Designated Network Resource barriers
bins.desc: SPP's one-to-one DNR requirement forces flexible loads to procure unnecessary generation capacity and impose unjust costs inconsistent with their actual operation. The requirement is prohibitively expensive, blocks beneficial load development, and contradicts cost-causation principles by treating differently-situated customers the same.
quotes: Highly flexible large loads face substantial interconnection barriers where, as in SPP, the RTO requires a one-to-one matching of non-coincident peak demand with new Designated Network Resources (DNRs). Highly flexible, curtailable large loads can avoid peaks and are inherently price sensitive, meaning they only operate when there is abundant low-cost energy generation available. An artificial DNR requirement is prohibitively expensive, unnecessary given the real-world operation of curtailable large loads like TES, and acts as a roadblock to new beneficial flexible loads
low load-factor, price-sensitive, highly flexible loads have no current mechanism for gaining transmission access without Designated Network Resources that will not be used or needed

Requiring such loads to procure generation capacity as though they consume around the clock yields unjust and unreasonable costs, contradicts cost-causation principles, and unduly discriminates against such loads by treating differently-situated customers the same

bins.name: Cost-causation principle violations
bins.desc: Antora argues that current SPP frameworks (DNRs and CHILLS) violate the Commission's cost-causation principle by charging flexible loads for capacity and transmission services based on assumed full-time operation rather than actual usage patterns. This discriminatory approach must be corrected via hourly, usage-based rates.
quotes: Requiring such loads to procure generation capacity as though they consume around the clock yields unjust and unreasonable costs, contradicts cost-causation principles, and unduly discriminates against such loads by treating differently-situated customers the same

This approach is unjust and unreasonable, inconsistent with the Commission's cost causation principle, and unduly discriminatory for treating differently situated loads similarly—as well as prohibitively expensive for highly price sensitive industrial loads like thermal storage

bins.name: CHILLS program limitations
bins.desc: While CHILLS represents innovation in non-firm service, Antora finds it insufficient: the seven-year term is temporary and requires transition to firm service, and annual reservation rates are misaligned with low load-factor usage patterns, imposing costs during low-LMP hours when the system benefits most from flexible load.
quotes: CHILLS is conditional and temporary, only available for a seven-year term, after which the customer must transition to firm service

CHILLS requires annual reservations. This means that an Antora thermal storage site would have to pay for transmission at the peak and off peak non-firm point to point rates (which can be very expensive) despite the fact that it would only use power ~1/3 of the time, and only during hours when LMPs are at their lowest

bins.name: Price Adaptive Load Service framework
bins.desc: PALS is SPP's emerging non-firm service model offering long-term, permanent transmission service for curtailable loads without capacity requirements. It features full curtailability, hourly MWh-based rates reflecting cost causation, bid price caps preventing price impacts, and elimination of resource adequacy complications. Antora considers PALS a sound model that should be adopted nationally.
quotes: Price Adaptive Load Service (PALS). SPP staff are currently developing the PALS proposal and plan to bring it to the stakeholder process for approvals later this year. PALS would create a long-term, non-firm transmission service enabling flexible, price-sensitive loads to interconnect without matching capacity

Full Curtailability: PALS loads are always dispatchable to zero. They are curtailed during reliability events, emergency conditions, and meaningful system peaks. SPP or the Transmission Owner can shut down PALS load immediately

Non-Firm Transmission Charges: Per-MWh billing at hourly non-firm rates (Zonal Annual Transmission Revenue Requirement/Transmission Zone Peak Load/8760) reflect the true cost causation profile of curtailable loads; directly supporting NITS costs reduction for existing ratepayers

PALS offers a sound model—protecting reliability through full curtailability, limiting price impacts through a bid cap, avoiding resource adequacy complications by design, and charging based on actual use

bins.name: Thermal energy storage commercial opportunity
bins.desc: Antora identifies 6+ GW of viable, cost-effective TES deployment in SPP and 10+ GW nationally, deployable at existing industrial facilities in biofuels, chemicals, food processing, and agriculture. Realizing this opportunity requires regulatory frameworks enabling flexible load interconnection.
quotes: Antora has identified a 6+ GW opportunity in SPP and 10+ GW nationally of immediately cost effective, viable, and highly-flexible thermal load opportunities based on current wholesale energy prices, which can be sited at existing facilities in biofuels, chemicals, food processing, and agriculture

bins.name: Recent RTO flexibility reforms
bins.desc: Antora acknowledges and applauds recent RTO efforts (CHILLS, PALS) to enable large flexible load interconnection. However, it argues these reforms fall short of complete solutions and should inform, but not limit, the Commission's national rulemaking to require comparable permanent, cost-causation-based non-firm service across all RTOs.
quotes: Antora submits these supplemental comments to provide Antora's commercial experience with these proposals, including the particular solutions that hold the most promise and the ways they fall short of complete solutions to enable and unleash the benefits of load flexibility

bins.name: FERC recommendations for all transmission providers
bins.desc: Antora calls on FERC to require all jurisdictional RTOs and transmission providers to offer permanent non-firm service for curtailable loads; set rates on cost-causation and actual usage principles; integrate curtailability into interconnection studies; eliminate DNRs and resource adequacy requirements for fully curtailable service; and study benefits of avoiding transmission upgrades.
quotes: Antora urges the Commission, in crafting its remedy in this proceeding, to go beyond these recent reforms to require all transmission providers to: (1) offer permanent non-firm transmission service; (2) at rates reflecting actual use that is consistent with cost causation principles; (3) with curtailment requirements for non-firm load integrated into the interconnection study process and resource adequacy requirements

Offer a permanent, long-term, non-firm transmission service for fully-curtailable, price-responsive loads in addition to temporary or conditional service for loads wishing to move to firm. Recognizing that some customers such as Antora can limit withdrawals whether or not co-located with generation, this service should be available to any customer capable of meeting the necessary withdrawal limitations, whether or not co-located
Set transmission charges based on cost-causation principles reflecting actual use that is consistent with how RTOs already bill other firm customers
Integrate the non-firm, curtailable nature of flexible loads into the interconnection study process so assumptions reflect actual operating profiles

bins.name: System benefits and cost reductions from flexible loads
bins.desc: Flexible TES deployment yields substantial system benefits: increased transmission utilization, price normalization during low-demand periods, reduced wholesale and capacity costs, and avoided transmission upgrade needs. One GW of flexible loads could generate USD 65.4 million in transmission revenues, reducing existing customer NITS fees by 1-12% in SPP zones.
quotes: Antora estimates 1 GW of flexible load projects could generate $65.4 million in additional transmission revenues, reducing existing customer NITS fees by 1-12% depending on the zone in SPP
Study the full range of benefits that efficiently-sited highly flexible loads can provide to the system, including by avoiding the need for new transmission upgrades

bins.name: Framework design flaws
bins.desc: Current interconnection and service frameworks assume all loads operate at full capacity around the clock, which is incompatible with flexible, price-sensitive loads that only operate during low-LMP periods. This fundamental design flaw hamstrings the commercial viability of beneficial flexible load deployment.
quotes: This opportunity is being hamstrung by frameworks that assume all loads operate at full capacity around the clock

bins.name: National non-firm service requirement
bins.desc: Antora urges FERC to require all jurisdictional RTOs and transmission providers to offer permanent, cost-causation-based non-firm service for flexible loads, not confined to SPP. A national mandate prevents investment distortion and ensures comparable, timely, orderly, and affordable interconnection across regions.
quotes: every jurisdictional RTO and transmission owner should be required to offer comparable, permanent, cost-causation-based non-firm service for flexible loads
